figures.caption: Figure 2: FID versus NFE for CIFAR-10 with VP/VE schedules and ImageNet with the VP schedule. CAB-2 and CAB-3 consistently improve over the corresponding AB baselines in the low-NFE regime. They remain competitive with, and often outperform, strong training-free fast samplers.

Figure 14: Prompt: “A fluffy grey and white cat is lazily stretched out on a sunny window sill, enjoying a nap after a long day of lounging.” Temporal comparison of training-free samplers on HunyuanVideo-1.5 over four frames from the same generated video. CAB-2 preserves sharper fur texture, clearer facial structure, and more consistent window-side lighting, while DPM++ and STORK appear blurrier and show weaker appearance consistency across frames.

Figure 13: Prompt: “light wind, feathers moving, she moves her gaze, 4k.” Temporal comparison of training-free samplers on HunyuanVideo-1.5 over four frames from the same generated video. CAB-2 better preserves appearance consistency and smoother motion progression, while DPM++ and STORK show stronger temporal drift and frame-to-frame variation.

Figure 15: Prompt: “a giraffe eating an apple.” Temporal comparison of training-free samplers on HunyuanVideo-1.5 over four frames from the same generated video. CAB-2 preserves cleaner appearance and more coherent scene context across frames, while DPM++ and STORK show weaker texture consistency and less stable background structure.
Table 6: Extension of Table. 6 including AB2 and AB3

Figure 9: Qualitative comparison of samplers in the 8-NFE regime for DiT. Increasing the solver order of DPM++ and STORK can lead to over-correction of the predictor trajectory, producing amplified artifacts and structural distortions in the Grey Fox image, consistent with the quantitative trends in Table 1. AB predictor algorithms generate images that oversmooth, whereas CAB-based methods maintain improved visual consistency and cleaner structural fidelity under the same sampling budget.
Figure 1: Qualitative comparison of training-free samplers on QWEN-Image 1024 × 1024 flow generation [18] and DiT 256× 256 VP diffusion generation [19].
Table 1: FID comparison on DiT/ImageNet 256× 256, where lower is better. Sampler-O3 denotes the corresponding third-order variant using three past velocity evaluations, analogous to CAB-3.
Figure 10: Unconditional generation on CIFAR-10 using EDM model.

Figure 7: Empirical verification of the accuracy results in Theorem 3.2 on two representative nonlinear velocity fields. (a) and (c) show sample trajectories and normalized velocity fields, while (b) and (d) plot the global maximum error against the maximum step size hmax on non-uniform ρ-grids. In both examples, the observed slopes match the theoretical predictions; CAB-2 exhibits second-order global accuracy, whereas CAB-3 exhibits third-order global accuracy .

Figure 16: Trajectories produced by AB2, AB3, CAB-2, and CAB-3, compared with the reference solution, for different sampling budgets. At very low step counts, particularly N = 6, the numerical trajectories can deviate substantially from the reference path, revealing geometric mis-tracking in challenging regions of the velocity field. As the number of sampling steps increases, the trajectories become better aligned with the reference.

Figure 8: Comparison of AB2/AB3 and the proposed CAB-2/CAB-3 on two representative nonlinear rectified ODEs with a small sampling budget (N = 8). The top row shows the numerical trajectories overlaid on the corresponding velocity fields, and the bottom row shows the trajectory error ∥ŷρ − ŷref(ρ)∥2 as a function of ρ. These visualizations highlight the benefit of the proposed correction in improving low-step integration accuracy to match the exact output.
